text: DERMATOLOGIC SURGERY The superiorly based bilobed flap for nasal reconstruction Michael Kelly-Sell, MD,a,b S. Tyler Hollmig, MD,c andJoelCook,MDa Charleston, South Carolina; Clinton Township, Michigan; and Redwood City, California Background: The laterally based bilobed ﬂap is commonly used for the reconstruction of small- to medium-sized defects of the distal portion of the nose; However, when this ﬂap is used to repair defects that are larger, more cephalic, or more lateral on the nose, there is a risk for lower nasal distortion. Reorienting the base superiorly preserves the advantages of the traditional design while minimizing this risk. Objective: To demonstrate the design, execution, and efﬁcacy of the superiorly based bilobed ﬂap. Methods: A retrospective review examined all superiorly based bilobed ﬂaps performed by 1 surgeon (J.C.) in 2000-2016 after tumor extirpation by Mohs micrographic surgery at a single institution. Results: A total of 41 surgical defects were closed with 40 ﬂaps between June 2000 and August 2016 (1 patient had 2 defects closed with a single ﬂap). Of the tumors, 55% were located on the nasal dorsum, and the median of the longest postoperative tumor axis was 1.4 cm. Follow-up was available for 40 ﬂaps, and no infections, hematomas, or episodes of full-thickness necrosis were observed. Limitations: Data were collected retrospectively from a single institution without a standardized assessment tool for aesthetic outcomes. Conclusion: The superiorly based bilobed ﬂap is useful for nasal reconstruction. ( J Am Acad Dermatol 2018;78:370-6.) Key Words: bilobed ﬂap; facial reconstruction; Mohs micrographic surgery; nasal reconstruction; superiorly based bilobed ﬂap; transposition ﬂap. T he laterally based bilobed ﬂap is commonly used for the aesthetic and functional repair of small- to medium-sized nasal defects of the supratip and lateral nasal tip.1,2 For these defects, the bilobed flap offers distinct advantages over alternative repairs: access to an adjacent tissue reservoir with similar aesthetic qualities, predictable flap viability, and the reduced morbidity of a single-stage procedure.3 In the traditional design, the vascular inlet, or base, of the bilobed flap is located laterally, where tributaries of the angular artery provide a robust vascular supply to nourish the flap.4 This design harvests the available lax skin of the nasal dorsum to repair more distal nasal defects and is the criterion standard in lower nasal repair for many surgeons. 1,4 However, with the traditional design, it may be a struggle to deliver reproducible aesthetic success for nasal defects with a diameter larger than 1.0 cm, wounds located on other parts of the nose, or patients with thick skin on the nasal dorsum. In such circumstances, the closure of the ﬂap’s terminal From the Department of Dermatology and Dermatologic Surgery, Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston a; Midwest Skin Cancer Surgery Center, Clinton Township b; and Department of Dermatology, Stanford University Medical Center, Redwood City. c Supported by the Department of Dermatology and Dermatologic Surgery, Medical University of South Carolina. Conflicts of interest: None declared. Presented at the 2017 American College of Mohs Surgery, San Francisco, CA; April, 27, 2017. Accepted for publication September 2, 2017. Reprints not available from the authors. Correspondence to: Michael Kelly-Sell, MD, Midwest Skin Cancer Surgery Center, 43900 Garfield Rd, Suite 129, Clinton Township, MI 48038. E-mail: mkellysell@dmsderm.com. Published online October 19, 2017. 0190-9622/$36.00  2017 by the American Academy of Dermatology, Inc. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaad.2017.09.014 370 donor site on the nasal dorsum or sidewall may introduce a signiﬁcant risk for permanent lower nasal distortion (Fig 1). To circumvent this problem, we reoriented the base (or pivot point) from a lateral to a superior location and moved the terminal donor site from the nose to the paranasal cheek. This preserves the geometric advantages of the traditional bilobed flap while harvesting an aesthetically similar donor site with less risk for anatomic distortion. Herein we report our experience with the superiorly based bilobed flap for the recon- struction of nasal defects. METHODS The Medical University of South Carolina Institutional Review Board approved this study (Pro00058644). We performed a retrospective review of all Mohs micro- graphic surgery defects re- paired with a bilobed ﬂap by a single surgeon (J.C.) after tumor clearance between June 2000 and August 2016. All Mohs micrographic surgical procedures were performed with clean technique, and all closures were performed with sterile technique.5 All Mohs micrographic surgical cases and subse- quent repairs during this period were recorded in a commercially available database (Microsoft Access, Microsoft Corporation, Redmond, WA, and Malachite Corporation, Durham, NC). The database was searched for all adult patients (18 years or older) who had a bilobed repair of a surgical defect on the nose. Postoperative photos were then reviewed to determine the location of the base (lateral, medial, superior, or inferior). All cases with a superiorly based design and documented follow-up were included in our study. Data were collected on tumor sublocation, tumor type, tumor size at presentation, postoperative defect size, postopera- tive prescriptions, and surgical complications, which included hematoma, infection, ﬂap necrosis, and nasal valve dysfunction (as recorded in the patients’ medical record during the 4 months after surgery). Follow-up photos taken at 4 months or later were reviewed for all ﬂaps to determine aesthetic results. As with any bilobed ﬂap, correct design is critical to achieve operative success. Flaps were designed as a random pattern transposition, as described by Zitelli, but with the vascular base, or pivot point, oriented superiorly toward the mid glabella or ipsilateral medial canthus (Fig 2).4 A Burow triangle from the surgical defect is directed toward the pivot point and should be both perpendicular to the alar margin (to avoid contralateral alar distortion on flap inset) and long enough to form a 30-degree angle, eliminating redundant skin at the point of rotation.6 The widths of the primary and secondary lobules are designed nearly equivalent to the width of the primary defect. This decreases sec- ondary motion and the concomitant risk for nasal distortion while also limiting the risk for pincushioning, which occurs more often when the lobules are over- sized. The primary lobule is designed on the part of the nose abutting the surgical defect, and the amount of skin available on the nose to create this lobule generally limits the size of the defect that can be repaired, as ex- tending this lobule onto the cheek could blunt the nasofacial sulcus. The secondary lobule is then placed on the paranasal cheek adjacent to the melolabial fold as in a nasolabial transposition flapdwith its inferior edge within the melolabial fold and a Burow triangle extending caudally.7 This placement ultimately hides the incision lines in the melolabial fold with minimal aesthetic penalty. The flap’s arc of rotation should be adjusted to place the secondary lobule as described, and as with all rotating flaps, the surgeon must account for some shortening of the lobules as a result of pivotal restraint. As in a traditional bilobed ﬂap, we excise the Burow triangle extending from the surgical defect ﬁrst to allow for more accurate placement of the primary lobule.6 The primary lobule is then incised and raised in the submuscular plane while beginning at the nasofacial sulcus, the secondary lobule is undermined in a subcutaneous plane. The entire surgical defect should be widely undermined to minimize the trapdoor effect.7 Hemostasis is attained with electrocoagulation, taking care to not damage the sebaceous nasal skin. The tertiary defect is first closed to ‘‘push’’ the primary and secondary lobules toward their desired positions. Use of a periosteal suspension suture to anchor the advancing cheek to the pyriform aperture may be helpful to decrease tension on the flap but is not typically required. The secondary lobule is then lifted into position, rather than slid, to avoid blunting the nasofacial sulcus. CAPSULE SUMMARY d The laterally based bilobed flap has anatomic restrictions that may limit its utility for nasal reconstruction. d Reorienting the base superiorly moves the terminal donor site off the nose and decreases the risk for lower nasal distortion. d The superiorly based bilobed flap preserves the advantages of the traditional design and expands the utility of this flap. JAM ACAD DERMATOL VOLUME 78, NUMBER 2 Kelly-Sell, Hollmig, and Cook 371 After closure of the tertiary defect, the flap’s lobules are inset with meticulous use of buried vertical mattress sutures and the epidermis is closed with a simple running suture.8 All surgical wounds were dressed with petrolatum, nonstick gauze, and surgical tape. Patients were instructed to change their dressings and clean their wounds daily. Sutures were generally removed 7 days after surgery. Patients were prescribed a 5-day course of a cephalosporin unless contraindicated, in which case clindamycin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole was generally substituted. Preoperative antibiotics were not typically prescribed unless the patient had had a joint replacement within the past year. RESULTS Our data base search found 1137 cases involving a bilobed ﬂap in an adult patient within the study period. Upon reviewing postoperative photos, we identiﬁed 41 superiorly based bilobed ﬂaps per- formed to repair 42 nasal defects (1 patient had 2 defects on the nose that were closed with 1 ﬂap). One ﬂap was excluded, as there was no documented follow-up in the medical record. The remaining 40 ﬂaps were performed between August 2012 and the end of the study period. Figure 3 shows the distribution of tumor sublocations, with the most common tumor locations being the nasal dorsum (55%), tip (18%), and ala (13%). The median of the longest preoperative tumor axis was 0.8 cm (range, 0.4-1.6 cm) and the median of the longest postoperative tumor axis measured 1.4 cm (range, 0.8-2.3 cm). In all, 14 surgical defects (35%) had a postoperative measurement larger than 1.5 cm. Medical records for the patients’ 1-week and 4-month postoperative ofﬁce visits with their surgeon (J.C.) were available for all 40 ﬂaps. Complications were uncommondwith no increased frequency versus that with traditionally oriented bilobed ﬂaps.9 There were no cases of infection, hematoma formation, or nasal valve dysfunction. No flap showed complete or full-thickness necrosis; 2 flaps showed superficial or partial distal flap necrosis, with 1 requiring a revisionary procedure. This patient was a former smoker and had a good final aesthetic outcome. This was the only revisionary flap performed in our series. The second case involved the formation of a superficial eschar on the distal flap that resolved with regular use of petrolatum and monitoring. Typical aesthetic outcomes for patients are shown in Figs 4 and 5, with any refinement procedures performed before the final outcome noted. Secondary procedures to fine-tune the aesthetic result were performed on a minority of flaps: Fig 1. Contralateral alar elevation after repair of a surgical defect on the lateral tip with a traditional bilobed ﬂap. A, Postsurgical defect. B, Postoperative repair. C and D, Five-year follow-up demonstrating permanent contralateral alar elevation. JAM ACAD DERMATOL FEBRUARY 2018 372 Kelly-Sell, Hollmig, and Cook 8 flaps were refined with dermabrasion in the office (20%), 3 flaps were treated with a 585-nm pulsed dye laser (8%), and 2 flaps had minor scar revisions (5%), such as the excision of a small (\1 cm) hypertrophic scar. These are similar to the rates reported for a traditional bilobed flap.9 Importantly, there was little distortion of lower nasal anatomy and no blunting of the nasofacial sulcus. We observed 1 case of iatrogenic alar elevation and 1 patient with prominent scarring and telangiectasias (Fig 6). These patients did not have secondary refinement procedures. DISCUSSION Herein, we have described our experience with 40 superiorly based bilobed ﬂaps used to repair 41 surgical defects on the nose. We found that this ﬂap is particularly useful to repair defects that are larger than 1.5 cm in diameter, located off the nasal tip, or located on patients with thick nasal skin. In our series, 14 defects (35%) had a postoperative measurement of their longest axis larger than 1.5 cm, which is commonly considered the upper limit for a defect to be repaired with a traditional bilobed ﬂap. However, by moving the terminal donor site to the medial cheek (which is a larger and more forgiving tissue reservoir), it is possible to reconstruct these defects with less risk for lower nasal distortion. Furthermore, with rotation of the pivot point, it was possible to use this ﬂap to repair many surgical defects located on the nasal dorsum, a location that a traditional design may struggle to repair on account of geometric and mechanical restraints. We believe that with some slight design modiﬁcations this, ﬂap may be used to repair defects located on the nasal root, which is an area for further study. Conversely, the superiorly based bilobed ﬂap is not well suited for repair of defects on the lower Fig 2. The design and raising of a superiorly based bilobed ﬂap. A, All lobules should be equivalent in width to the surgical defect, with the secondary lobule placed abutting the melolabial fold. B, The primary lobule is raised in a submuscular plane, whereas the secondary lobule is raised in a subcutaneous plane. The area surrounding the flap should be undermined, as marked. w/i, Within. Figure courtesy of Gregory Jacobs. All rights reserved. Fig 3. Numbers of tumors located at each nasal sublocation. Figure courtesy of Gregory Jacobs. All rights reserved. JAM ACAD DERMATOL VOLUME 78, NUMBER 2 Kelly-Sell, Hollmig, and Cook 373 Fig 4. Surgical repairs utilizing a superiorly based bilobed ﬂap. Postsurgical defects measuring 1 3 1cm (A), 1.2 3 1.1 cm (E), and 1.2 3 1.3 cm (I). B, F, and J, Postoperative repair. C, D, G, H, K, and L, Final aesthetic outcome. Patient shown in (A) through (D) was treated with pulsed dye laser. Fig 5. Surgical repairs utilizing a superiorly based bilobed ﬂap. Postsurgical defects measuring 0.8 3 0.8 cm (A), 1.8 3 1.7 cm (E), and 1.6 3 1.7 cm (I). B, F, and J, Postoperative repair. C, D, G, H, K, and L, Final aesthetic outcome. Patient shown in (E) through (H) was treated with dermabrasion. JAM ACAD DERMATOL FEBRUARY 2018 374 Kelly-Sell, Hollmig, and Cook nasal tip and infratip, as pivotal restraint would require the primary lobule to extend onto the alar rim, which is unacceptable. Finally, we have found that this ﬂap is useful in patients with tight nasal skin, where closure of a terminal donor site on the nose may be challenging and options for local ﬂap repairs are limited. Harvesting skin from the medial cheek, which often has some laxity, and using the decreased arc of rotation from the bilobed design allows these nasal defects to be closed with a local ﬂap. Alternative repair techniques may be used for many of the wounds illustrated in this article, but we think that they are less than ideal for a variety of reasons. Sliding ﬂaps, in particular, the cheek advancement ﬂap, are often used to repair defects on the nasal sidewall, but they tend to bridge the Fig 6. Potential aesthetic complications of a superiorly based bilobed ﬂap. Postsurgical defects measuring 1.1 3 1.1 cm (A) and 1.2 3 1.4 cm (E). B, and F, Postoperative repair. C and D, Final aesthetic outcome showing prominent scarring with telangiectasias. G and H, Final aesthetic outcome showing ipsilateral alar elevation. Fig 7. Blunting of the nasofacial sulcus and depression of the ipsilateral ala after repair with a cheek advancement ﬂap. A, Postsurgical defect. B, Postoperative repair. C and D, Final aesthetic outcome. JAM ACAD DERMATOL VOLUME 78, NUMBER 2 Kelly-Sell, Hollmig, and Cook 375 nasofacial sulcus when used for wounds that are either too large or are located too far anteriorly on the nose. These ﬂaps can cause a visually distracting loss of the shadowed concavity delineating the cheek and nose as separate cosmetic subunits, even when appropriate periosteal tacking sutures are utilized. Furthermore, the pushing effect of the ﬂap may cause ipsilateral alar depression (Fig 7). The nasolabial transposition flap is best suited for defects located on the lateral nose, as the arc of rotation to reach the nasal dorsum and supratip requires an excessively long flap that is quite prone to development of trapdoor deformity or distal necrosis. 7 The superiorly based bilobed flap mobilizes the same tissue reservoir with a smaller arc of rotation. Dorsal nasal rotation flaps serve as a reasonable single-stage alternative for centrally located wounds of the supratip and dorsum, but they are prone to elevate the nasal tip when used for defects with a sizeable horizontal component. These wounds are typically more easily addressed with a tissue reservoir located lateral to, rather than above, the primary defect. The trilobed flap is an excellent reconstructive option for distal defects on the nasal tip and ala, but we believe that it is not ideal for more proximal defects on the nasal dorsum. 10 Finally, although interpolation flaps, such as the paramedian forehead flap, are the criterion standard for complex nasal reconstruction, these surgical procedures are demanding on patients and require multiple procedures. As with any reconstructive technique, this ﬂap has potential limitations. Flap design and suture technique must be meticulous, because as with other round ﬂaps, there may be a tendency to ‘‘trapdoor’’ and create a visible scar along the inferior margins of the ﬂap. Using primary and secondary lobules that are nearly equivalent in size to the surgical defect and suturing so that these lobules sink concavely into surrounding coarse nasal skin can almost always avoid this complication. Furthermore, during repair of distal defects, the alar groove may be blunted if the primary lobule extends onto the body of the ala. For these defects, we place a tacking suture on the underside of the ﬂap that recreates the alar groove at the correct anatomic position. This suture should be oriented vertically on the underside of the ﬂap so as not to constrict the blood supply.7 In summary, we have demonstrated that the superiorly based bilobed ﬂap offers reproducibly excellent results for nasal reconstruction. By changing the orientation of the vascular pedicle and placing the terminal donor site on the medial cheek, this ﬂap allows for the reconstruction of nasal defects with signiﬁcantly less risk for lower nasal distortion. We believe that this design modiﬁcation expands the utility of the bilobed ﬂap for nasal reconstruction and complements the traditional design. The authors would like to thank Mr Gregory Jacobs for his help in designing and creating the illustrations for this manuscript. REFERENCES 1. Burget GC, Menick FJ. Repair of small surface defects.
